name: Italy
summary: Cheap public MSc (Polimi non-EU ~€3,891/yr), an acute IT/AI skills shortage and Europe's largest Bangladeshi community (~175k-200k) make Italy a viable study->work->PR route for two engineers, but the visa is adjudicated in New Delhi not Dhaka, naturalisation stays at 10 years (June 2025 5-year referendum failed) with B1 Italian, tech salaries lag Northern Europe, and the Meloni government is hard-right on irregular migration.

links.title: Universitaly - official study-in-Italy portal
Ambasciata d'Italia Dhaka - Consular Services & Visas (official)

job-market.summary: Digital/IT roles hardest-to-fill 3yrs running; AI engineering top-demand role; both hireable, but Italian tech pay lags peers.
job-market.reasoning: Software developers, cybersecurity, data and AI engineers are in acute shortage across Italy, with AI ranked the most in-demand profession (machine-learning demand reportedly +625%) and hiring concentrated in Milan, Rome and Turin. The €191.5bn PNRR (must be disbursed by Aug 2026) keeps driving digitisation hiring, so both engineers are easily absorbable in a two-income household. The drag is structurally low pay versus Germany/Nordics and Italian-language requirements at smaller firms, which caps the score.
evidence: Software devs, cybersecurity, data analysts in short supply; 44% of manufacturers can't fill technical engineering vacancies (2026)
AI Engineer ranked most in-demand profession; ML job demand surge cited at +625%
PNRR allocates €191.5bn EU recovery funds, disbursement deadline Aug 2026, fuelling tech hiring
55% of Italian companies adopting cloud; demand high in big firms and startups alike
Market competitive (many applicants); Italian often expected outside big-city hubs

links.title: Italy's IT Job Market 2026: Trends and Opportunities (JobTrackr)
Top 10 In-Demand Skills in Italy for 2026 (Edstellar)

visa-access.summary: Filed at VFS Dhaka (also Chattogram/Sylhet) but adjudicated by Italian Embassy New Delhi; low funds bar is the bright spot.
visa-access.reasoning: Italy has an embassy in Dhaka but national type-D study visas are filed exclusively through VFS Global (Dhaka, Chattogram, Sylhet) and forwarded to the Italian Embassy in New Delhi for adjudication, not decided locally - a genuine penalty under the Bangladesh lens. The financial bar is modest (~€534.41/month) and there is no blocked-account requirement, but New-Delhi routing, document translation/legalisation into Italian and noisy/unofficial refusal data hold this in the mixed band. Approval-ratio figures for Bangladeshi students are not officially published and vary widely by consultant (~12-15% rejection in some sources, much higher in others).
evidence: Visas filed exclusively at VFS Global (Dhaka, Chattogram, Sylhet) - Embassy Dhaka official
Files forwarded to the Italian Embassy in New Delhi for decision (no local adjudication)
Financial proof ~€534.41/month; no blocked account required
Documents must be translated into Italian and legalised; pre-enrolment via Universitaly required
BD student approval ratios not officially published; rejection estimates range ~12-15% to much higher

links.title: Visas - Ambasciata d'Italia Dhaka (official)
Student procedures - Ambasciata d'Italia New Delhi (official)

citizenship.summary: 10yr residence + B1 Italian; June 2025 referendum to cut to 5yr FAILED (30.6% turnout); but dual citizenship fully allowed.
citizenship.reasoning: Naturalisation for a non-EU national still requires 10 years' continuous legal residence plus B1 Italian and stable income - one of the longest timelines in this set, and the June 2025 referendum to halve it to 5 years failed for lack of quorum (only 30.6% turnout vs 50%+1 needed), so the 10-year path is locked in. The decisive plus for this couple is that Italy fully recognises dual citizenship - they would not renounce Bangladeshi nationality. The 2025 Law 74 reform tightened citizenship by descent only; it does not affect the naturalisation timeline for Bangladeshis.
evidence: 10 years' continuous legal residence for non-EU naturalisation (unchanged)
June 2025 referendum to cut to 5 years FAILED: 30.6% turnout vs 50% quorum (Wikipedia/NPR)
B1 Italian (CEFR) mandatory; stable income + clean record required
Dual citizenship fully permitted - no renunciation of Bangladeshi nationality
Law 74/2025 only changed citizenship-by-descent rules, not the 10-year path

links.title: 2025 Italian referendum (Wikipedia, sourced)
Italian Citizenship by Residency: Requirements and 2025 Updates (Boccadutri)

post-study-work.summary: 12-month job-search permit (attesa occupazione); no quota cap on study->work conversion since 2023; non-renewable, shorter than peers.
post-study-work.reasoning: Italian-university graduates can convert their study permit to a 12-month residence permit for job-seeking/self-employment (permesso per attesa occupazione) and then to a work permit once employed, and since the 2023 Cutro Decree these study-to-work conversions face no annual quota cap - a real plus. Both partners as Italian graduates can each hold one, but the 12 months is non-renewable and shorter than the 18-24 month or multi-year stay-back of Germany, NZ or Canada, capping the score.
evidence: 12-month job-search permit (attesa occupazione) after an Italian degree; start switch 2 months before study permit expires
Convertible to a work permit once an employer is found
No annual quota restriction on study->work conversions since the 2023 Cutro Decree
Non-renewable; must secure sponsorship within the year
Both graduates can each hold their own permit

links.title: Job Seeking Permit (Permesso Attesa Occupazione) - Bocconi University
Permesso di Soggiorno for employment search (attesa occupazione) - ItaliaHello

spouse-family.summary: Both can enrol on own student visas (each works 20h/wk); family permit (motivi familiari) allows work - no UK-style dependant ban.
spouse-family.reasoning: Because both are CS graduates, the cleanest route is each enrolling on their own student visa, with each working part-time (20h/week, 1,040h/year) during study and holding their own post-study permit. Alternatively a spouse joining via family reunification gets a permit that allows work and study - Italy has no taught-master's dependant work ban (unlike the UK). The friction: family-reunification sponsorship now needs ~2 years' prior continuous residence (raised from 1 year), so the dual-student route is preferable.
evidence: Each spouse can hold an independent student visa + work 20h/week (1,040h/yr); self-employment uncapped
Family-reunification residence permit holders may work and study freely
No UK-style ban on dependants of taught-master's students working
Family-reunification sponsorship now needs ~2 years' continuous residence (up from 1 yr), per 2024 amendment
Sponsor income floor ~€6,700/yr for one member, +~€2,300-3,000 per extra adult

links.title: Italy: Stricter Continuous Residence Rules for Sponsoring Family Members (Fragomen)
Residence permit for study purposes - work rights (Sapienza Università di Roma, official)

msc-study.summary: 30+ English MSc at Polimi; CS&Eng non-EU tuition only ~€3,891/yr, scholarships, 20h/wk work, ~€10k/yr living, spouse can join.
msc-study.reasoning: Politecnico di Milano offers 30+ English-taught graduate programmes including a 2-year MSc in Computer Science & Engineering with non-EU annual tuition of only ~€3,891 for 2025/26 and a broad scholarship system. Students may work 20h/week (1,040h/year) and a spouse can accompany or enrol separately. It misses the top band only because non-EU students without scholarships pay near the maximum (no ISEE reduction) and English MSc breadth concentrates in a few strong universities; living costs run ~€10,000/year.
evidence: Polimi: 30+ English-taught graduate programmes incl. CS & Engineering (2-yr, English)
Non-EU MSc tuition ~€3,891/year for CS&Eng (2025/26); range ~€3,900-4,000
Broad scholarship system; many regional DSU/DSU-Lombardia grants
Students may work 20h/week, 1,040 hours/year during study
Estimated living costs ~€10,000/year (accommodation, food, transport)

links.title: Polimi tuition fees - Laurea Magistrale (official)
Universitaly - study in Italy (official)

pr-pathway.summary: EU long-term residence at 5 years + A2 Italian; study-permit time counts 100% (per official FVG), so MSc years count fully.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Italy's EU long-term residence permit needs 5 years' continuous legal residence (absences not exceeding 6 months continuous / 10 months total), income at least the assegno sociale, and A2 Italian (waived for Italian university graduates). Crucially, official regional guidance (Welcome Office FVG) states study-permit periods are counted at 100% toward the five years - correcting the earlier assumption of a 50% discount - so both MSc years count fully, making the headline 5-year timeline realistic for this couple. Predictable and clear, lifted by the A2-waiver for graduates.
evidence: EU long-term residence after 5 years' continuous legal residence
Study-permit periods counted at 100% toward the 5 years (Welcome Office FVG, official regional)
Income at least the assegno sociale (~€6,950/yr) required
A2 Italian test required, but WAIVED for holders of an Italian university degree
Absences must not exceed 6 months continuous / 10 months total over the 5 years

links.title: EU long-term residence permit (Welcome Office FVG, official regional)
The Italian long-term residence permit (Arletti Partners)

income-cost.summary: Milan SW median ~€38.5-48k gross (entry ~€23-33k) vs €1,150-1,500 one-bed rent; rent can be >70% of one net salary.
income-cost.reasoning: Milan software-engineer pay is roughly €38,500-48,000 gross median (entry-level ~€23,000-33,000; ML engineers ~€70k), well below Germany or the Nordics, while a one-bed runs €1,150-1,500/month (€1,500-2,200 central) and can exceed 70% of a single average net salary. Net pay typically sits ~€1,400-1,950/month and a comfortable single standard needs ~€2,500-3,000 net. Two engineering incomes plus cheaper non-central living enable modest savings, but weak gross and high housing keep net savings limited - the impatriati regime is the main lever.
evidence: Milan SW median ~€38,500-48,000 gross; entry-level ~€23,000-33,000; ML eng ~€70k
Milan one-bed €1,150-1,500/month (€1,500-2,200 central); rent can exceed 70% of one net salary
Typical Milan net salary ~€1,400-1,950/month; comfortable single needs ~€2,500-3,000 net
Non-rent living ~€1,050/month for a single person
Two incomes + non-central living enable modest savings; salaries lag Germany/Nordics

links.title: Software Engineer Salary in Milan (Glassdoor)

healthcare.summary: Universal SSN, high quality; free for workers/family, ~€700/yr voluntary for students (up from €149.77) - good but newcomer fee gap.
healthcare.reasoning: The Servizio Sanitario Nazionale (SSN) offers universal, high-quality coverage; enrolment is free and mandatory for work-permit holders (via employer) and family-reunification members, giving the couple full GP access once working. During study, voluntary SSN enrolment costs a non-fractionable ~€700/year per person (raised from €149.77 in 2024 under Law 213/2023), a modest newcomer cost. Quality and access are strong; once enrolled, benefits equal those of Italian citizens, but north-south regional disparities and the student fee hike keep it just below the top band.
evidence: SSN universal; free mandatory enrolment for workers (via employer) and family-reunification members
Students: voluntary SSN enrolment €700/year per person, non-fractionable (general non-EU rate €2,000)
Student fee rose from €149.77 to €700 in 2024 (Law 213/2023)
Registration needs a residence permit >3 months; GP assigned, benefits equal to citizens
North-south regional quality disparities persist

links.title: Annual SSN fee for non-EU nationals (Mazzeschi Legal Counsels)
Voluntary registration - Health (Welcome Office FVG, official)

culture-language.summary: EF EPI 2025: Italy 59th, lowest in EU, 'low proficiency' (dropped 13 places); Italian needed for officialdom, A2 PR / B1 citizenship.
culture-language.reasoning: Italy fell 13 places to 59th in the EF English Proficiency Index 2025 (score 513/700), now the lowest in the EU and in EF's 'low proficiency' band - materially worse for English-only newcomers than Northern Europe. English works in Milan's international tech firms and English MSc programmes, but daily bureaucracy and most non-hub workplaces require Italian, and the milestones demand it (A2 for PR, B1 for citizenship). High livability (climate, food, culture, city openness) partly offsets, but the language bar plus the EPI slide pull this into the lower-mixed band.
evidence: EF EPI 2025: Italy 59th, score 513/700 - lowest in the EU, 'low proficiency' band (down 13 places)
Regional spread: best Friuli-VG 553, Emilia-Romagna 534; lowest Calabria 482
Italian required for bureaucracy and most non-hub workplaces
A2 Italian for PR (waived for Italian graduates); B1 Italian for citizenship
High livability: climate, food, culture, city openness partly offset

links.title: Italy drops to bottom of EU ranking for English skills (The Local, EF EPI 2025)
EF English Proficiency Index 2025 (EF, official)

safety-law.summary: WJP 2025: 34th of 143 - mid-tier rule of law; slow civil justice; hard-line migrant enforcement but independent judiciary.
safety-law.reasoning: Italy ranks 34th of 143 in the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025, clearly mid-tier and well below Germany or the Nordics (top tier led by Denmark/Norway/Finland), dragged by slow civil justice and corruption perceptions. Violent crime is moderate and the judiciary is independent - it repeatedly blocked the Meloni government's Albania migrant-transfer scheme - but the government's hard-line enforcement signals a tougher legal climate for migrants, and petty theft is notable in tourist cities.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: Italy 34th of 143 (mid-tier; top tier Denmark/Norway/Finland/Sweden/NZ)
Slow civil justice and corruption perceptions weigh on the score
Independent judiciary repeatedly blocked Albania migrant-transfer scheme
Moderate violent crime; petty theft notable in tourist cities
Index measures 8 factors incl. Order & Security and Civil/Criminal Justice

politics.summary: Stable Meloni govt; Decreto Flussi 2026-28 ~500k permits with Bangladesh among 14 priority countries, but hard-right migration tone.
politics.reasoning: The Meloni government (since Sept 2022) is among Italy's most durable postwar administrations, a rare stretch of stability, and the 2026-2028 Decreto Flussi authorises ~500,000 work permits (164,850 in 2026) explicitly prioritising 14 partner countries that INCLUDE Bangladesh - a structural, profile-specific upside. The counterweight: it is a hard-right coalition focused on deterring irregular arrivals (Albania scheme, sea-arrival crackdowns), creating a chillier overall climate for migrants even as legal channels widen.
evidence: Meloni government in office since Sept 2022; durable, near-record postwar tenure
Decreto Flussi 2026-2028: ~500,000 work permits; 164,850 for 2026 (+11% vs prior plan)
Priority for 14 partner countries INCLUDING Bangladesh (also Pakistan, Egypt, Morocco, Albania)
Employer 'click-day' applications via ALI portal; demand outstrips supply in minutes
Hard-line on irregular migration (Albania transfers, sea-arrival crackdowns) cools overall tone

links.title: Italian Decreto Flussi 2026: Work Visa Quotas & Click-days (ILF Law Firm)
Government's New Decreto Flussi Opens 500,000 Work Permits for 2026-28 (VisaHQ)

tax.summary: IRPEF 23/33/43% + ~10% INPS + regional surtax, but impatriati regime taxes only 50% (60% with kids) of income for 5 years.
tax.reasoning: IRPEF is 23% to €28,000, 33% to €50,000 and 43% above €50,001, plus ~9-10% INPS social contributions and regional/municipal surtaxes, giving an effective burden of ~30-40% for SW salaries without relief. The decisive offset for new arrivals is the lavoratori impatriati regime: only 50% of qualifying Italian-source income is taxed for 5 years (60% if relocating with minor children; income cap €600,000), materially lifting take-home in the couple's first post-study years. Caveat: it now requires 'highly qualified' work, genuine residence transfer, no Italian tax residence in the prior 3 years, and a binding stay commitment with clawback.
evidence: INPS employee social contributions ~9-10%; regional/municipal surtaxes on top
Effective burden ~30-40% for SW salaries without relief
Impatriati regime: only 50% of income taxed for 5 years (60% with minor children), cap €600k
Requires highly-qualified work, no IT tax residence prior 3 yrs, genuine transfer + clawback if leaving early

links.title: Italy - Individual taxes on personal income (PwC Tax Summaries)
Impatriate Tax Regime Italy 2026 - new rules & benefits (Arletti Partners)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Largest Bangladeshi community in the EU (~175k-200k) + ~2.7M Muslims (4.9%); Bangla networks/halal abundant in cities, but ~8 official mosques and no Islam Intesa.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Italy hosts ~175,000-200,000 Bangladeshis - the largest Bangladeshi community in the EU (2nd in Europe after the UK), concentrated in Lazio/Rome (Tor Pignattara), Lombardy/Milan and the Veneto, and now a top-3 nationality for new residence permits - so Bangla networks, halal food and prayer spaces are abundant in cities, the decisive positive for this Bangladeshi household and stronger than smaller-diaspora peers. The drag is institutional rather than communal: ~2.7M Muslims (4.9%) are served by only ~8 officially recognised mosques (worship mostly in informal prayer rooms), Islam has no state Intesa, and the far-right government keeps the climate cooler - though everyday hate-crime rates stay relatively low, keeping this in the upper-middle band.
evidence: Bangladeshi community ~175,000-200,000 - largest in the EU (Italian ambassador, 2024); top-3 nationality for new permits
Concentrated in Lazio/Rome (Tor Pignattara), Lombardy/Milan, Veneto; remittances to BD >€1bn in 2023
~2.7 million Muslims (~4.9% of population, Jan 2025); halal food common in larger cities
Only ~8 officially recognised mosques; most worship in informal prayer rooms
No state Intesa with Islam (2017 National Pact never formalised); far-right govt cools sentiment, hate-crime rates relatively low
